summary: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report.

result.question:
For the Kaggle competition "ROGII Wellbore Geology Prediction" (kaggle.com/competitions/rogii-wellbore-geology-prediction; task: predict True Vertical Thickness (TVT) for horizontal wells from gamma-ray logs + trajectory; metric RMSE in feet; public LB top ~6.7; best published notebook ~7.776): find concrete, publicly documented techniques to reduce RMSE below a current 8.131 baseline. The baseline = a 6-model LightGBM/CatBoost stack (231 features: multi-scale NCC, particle filter, beam/Viterbi, DTW, formation plane-fit + row KNN imputation) output-blended with a 128-seed likelihood-weighted particle filter at weight 0.57.

Research angles, in priority order:
(1) This competition's forum discussions, code notebooks, and any writeups disclosing methods beyond the published ~7.776 "ridge-sp/sel15/sp45" family — especially anything attributable to the unpublished sub-7 leaders (SaintLouis, Tucker Arrants, Jacoby Jaeger) or new notebooks/topics published since early June 2026.
(2) Output-level blend/ensemble weighting schemes that transfer to hidden test sets: per-well or per-row adaptive blend weights, weight selection under few leaderboard probes, ensembling sequence-alignment estimators with GBMs at the output level rather than as features.
(3) Post-processing of predicted TVT/drift trajectories with quantified gains: per-well smoothing (Savitzky-Golay variants), drift shrinkage, fade-in from the prediction-start point, physics/monotonicity constraints, per-well bias re-calibration.
(4) Geosteering / well-log correlation / stratigraphic alignment literature (SPE papers, arXiv) on combining particle filters, Viterbi/HMM, cross-correlation, and kriging estimators, with quantified accuracy numbers.

Known dead-ends from our own scored submissions — do NOT re-recommend these: adding engineered features to the GBM stack (4 of 5 OOF gains inverted on the hidden LB), universal-kriging ANCC features, dip/curvature features, CWT detail-band texture features, RGT global warping (laterals are single-layer), transductive pooling of test wells, per-well selector routing between PF and GBM (gated null), PF spread-4.5 variant (null). The only axis that ever transferred favorably on the hidden set is output-level blending of the per-well, non-density-coupled particle filter with the GBM stack (blend weight already LB-mapped: 0.44→8.269, 0.57→8.158 vertex, 0.60→8.164, 0.77→8.429).

Prioritize sources specific to this competition, then analogous Kaggle sequence-alignment competitions, then domain literature. For each claimed technique, extract the quantified gain, what it was measured on (CV vs public LB vs hidden/private), and whether the data channels it needs exist in this competition (test wells expose only MD/X/Y/Z/GR/TVT_input — no resistivity, no formation tops at inference).
result.summary: No publicly documented technique attributable to the sub-7 ROGII leaders surfaced; the only published in-competition family beyond the baseline's lineage is (a) a hill-climbed 5-GBM + ANCC-PF blend with an exponential fade-in post-processing step and (b) a TCN starter at CV 15.5 that is non-competitive. The most actionable verified levers are: (1) the exponential drift fade-in from prediction start (d *= 1-exp(-md_since/85)) — a concrete post-processing transform not in the 8.131 baseline, corroborated by independent evidence that flat last-known-TVT continuation beats slope extrapolation; (2) replacing greedy/grid blend-weight search with a heavily regularized learned blend (ridge stacker over OOF predictions), which in an analogous Kaggle case beat hill-climbing on the hidden LB despite lower CV; and (3) per-group (per-well) adaptive blend weights via Feature-Weighted Linear Stacking, with a quantified +0.5%-absolute hidden-test gain that was decisive for an LSHTC4 win — but only under extreme meta-model regularization. Domain literature validates the baseline's PF/Viterbi-on-GR family as state of practice (GR-only is sufficient; the inversion is ill-posed, structurally favoring multi-interpretation output blending over any single MAP estimate) but offers no buildable new estimator given the competition's MD/X/Y/Z/GR/TVT_input-only test channels. Leaderboard probing is formally a dead end for hidden-set RMSE gains.

findings.claim: No published path below the 8.131 baseline exists in this competition's public notebooks, and nothing attributable to the unpublished sub-7 leaders (SaintLouis, Tucker Arrants, Jacoby Jaeger) was disclosed. The newest published family beyond the known ridge-sp lineage is Deotte's dilated 1D CNN/TCN starter (5 residual blocks, hidden 96, GroupKFold-by-well, residual over flat last-known-TVT), which scores only ~15.5 CV RMSE — roughly 2x the baseline — and barely beats its own flat baseline (15.91 -> 15.54). The published NN sequence-model family offers no direct path below 8.131.
findings.evidence: Kernel pulled via Kaggle API; executed log prints 'OOF NN RMSE: 15.53564' and 'Mean fold RMSE: 15.49900'. Counter-evidence search found no published NN/TCN notebook for this competition below ~9.2 LB; best published notebooks overall remain non-NN at ~7.8-9.25.

findings.claim: The one concrete, in-competition post-processing technique not in the 8.131 baseline is an exponential fade-in of predicted drift from the prediction-start point: ravaghi's hill-climbing notebook multiplies the blended drift by (1 - exp(-md_since/tau)) with tau=85 MD units and alpha=1.0 (no additional shrinkage), reconstructing predictions as last_known_tvt + faded drift. No quantified gain is disclosed (cell outputs stripped). Independent corroboration for the drift-shrinkage direction: Deotte found the flat last-known-TVT continuation to be the strongest residual baseline, demoting his earlier slope-continuation extrapolations to mere input features — evidence that shrinking drift toward zero slope beyond prediction start beats linear slope extrapolation.
findings.evidence: Verified verbatim in pulled kernel code: apply_pp() with d *= (1.-np.exp(-np.maximum(df['md_since'].values,0.)/tau)); pp_params = {'alpha': 1.0, 'tau': 85, 'w_pf': 0.09}. md_since = MD minus MD of last known TVT_input row. Deotte cell 6: 'The model predicts a residual from the strong flat last-known baseline'; v3 slope baselines kept only as features. Confidence medium because the gain is unquantified and flat-beats-slope rests on revealed preference (2-1 vote), not a head-to-head number.

findings.claim: The optimal PF output-blend weight is strongly PF-variant-dependent, not a transferable constant: ravaghi's notebook blends its ANCC-anchored 600-particle PF drift at w_pf=0.09 — an order of magnitude below the baseline's LB-mapped 0.57 vertex. A sibling kernel (beicicc 'WPF007') independently corroborates the ~0.07-0.09 operating region for that PF variant. Part of the small weight may reflect the GBM already absorbing that PF's signal via pf_ancc input features, but the baseline stack also feeds PF features to its GBM yet maps to 0.57, so non-transferability stands. Implication: do not import published w_pf constants; the baseline's own LB-mapped curve (0.44->8.269, 0.57->8.158, 0.60->8.164, 0.77->8.429) remains the only valid map for its PF variant.
findings.evidence: Verified in pulled kernel: pf_oof = train_df['pf_ancc'].values - base; ANCC_N=600; d = md*(1-w_pf) + pd_*w_pf with w_pf=0.09. Sibling kernel beicicc/rogii-dwt-fixed-tau25-wpf007-02 corroborates the 0.07-0.09 region.

findings.claim: For blend-weight selection that must transfer to a hidden test set, a heavily regularized learned blend beats greedy weight search: in Kaggle Playground S5E12, a ridge regression stacker (Top-36 hill-climbing-selected models, alpha=10, fit on rank-transformed predictions) broke a hill-climbing plateau and won 1st place — despite slightly LOWER CV than hill climbing (0.70860 vs 0.70886) — winning on public and private LB. A companion lesson from the same writeup: an alternative config (Top-34, alpha=5) scored better on the private LB (0.70514) but was passed over for a 0.00005-lower public LB — fine-grained blend configs ranked by small public-LB deltas can invert on the hidden set, so small public deltas should not drive weight selection. The ravaghi ROGII notebook's ensemble layer is the unlearned alternative: a single global hill-climbing weight vector (hill_climbing Climber, negative weights allowed, 0.001 precision) over 5 GBM OOF predictions applied unchanged to test.
findings.evidence: Writeup verbatim: 'The Ridge Ensemble (based on Top-36 models from HC and alpha = 10) had slightly lower post-cutoff CV than HC (0.70860 vs 0.70886), but performed better on both Public and Private LB'; 'Private LB of 0.70514, but the Public LB score of this ensemble was too low (0.70734)'. Caveats: public-LB margin over HC was tiny (+0.00017); rank transform is classification/AUC-specific and does not port literally to RMSE; the ridge winner could not have been selected by OOF improvement alone — directly relevant to ROGII's observed OOF->LB inversions.

findings.claim: Per-group adaptive output-blend weights CAN beat static weights by a margin-relevant amount, but the gain is fragile to meta-feature distribution shift: the LSHTC4 winning solution replaced fixed majority voting with per-label blend weights predicted by meta-feature regression (Feature-Weighted Linear Stacking variant; one linear regression per base model, targets = 'oracle' weights distributing 1 uniformly over the best base models per label on a held-out dev set, 55 meta-features). Gain: ~0.5% absolute macro-F on the hidden test, exceeding the 0.36-point winning margin (anttip 0.33931 vs 0.33567). Transfer caveat from the paper itself: only heavily regularized linear regression (ridge=1000) worked; richer meta-models failed due to overfitting, and meta-features shifted between the 23k dev set and 452k test set. ROGII analog: per-well FWLS weights between PF and GBM arms from well-level meta-features (e.g., GR match quality, lateral length, PF particle spread) — note the simpler per-well selector already gated null, so any retry must be the soft-weight FWLS form with extreme regularization and a pre-registered gate.
findings.evidence: arXiv:1405.0546 Section 6 verbatim: 'The use of metafeature regression in the ensemble instead of majority voting provided a moderate improvement of about 0.5%, and this much was needed for the win.' Section 4: 'regression models are trained separately for each base-classifier, using reference weights that approximate optimal weights per label in a development set.' Final LSHTC leaderboard pulled via Kaggle CLI confirms the margin. 2014 multilabel classification setting; per-well analogy is the researcher's mapping, not the source's.

findings.claim: Choosing among blend/post-processing variants under a limited probe budget has a formal framework with empirical support: Successive Halving / best-arm identification selects the best of K candidate output corrections with excess risk decaying exponentially in per-arm budget (explicit sufficient budget T >= 2(2L^2max/Delta^2 log(2Delta/eps) + 1) for K=2). Empirically (time-series forecasting benchmark), the correction toolbox is simple 1D output transforms (amplitude scaling, quantile rescaling, linear trend/intercept), the choice of bandit selector barely matters (LUCB +7.72%, SH +7.62%, SR +7.53%, Uniform +7.32% avg MSE improvement, overlapping error bars), and only one negligible negative result occurred across the full grid (-0.07%+/-0.41%). Practical ROGII reading: the candidate set (e.g., fade-in tau values, drift-shrinkage alphas, blend weights) matters far more than the selection algorithm, and simple multiplicative/affine drift corrections are rarely harmful in-distribution — but the i.i.d.-sample bandit framing maps only loosely onto deterministic single-split Kaggle LB probes.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2505.15354v3 (2026-06-03) verified by full-PDF extraction: Theorem B (Hoeffding + union bound), Corollary B for K=2; 5-stage pipeline, 50 evaluations per stage; selector numbers verbatim. Caveats: non-peer-reviewed preprint, self-reported benchmarks; Theorem B assumes oracle per-arm hyperparameters and i.i.d. loss samples; a related claim that gains for already-strong models are sub-1% was REFUTED in verification, so do not use this paper to bound expected gain magnitude on the 8.131 stack. ROGII's own history of validation-selected corrections inverting on the hidden LB applies.

findings.claim: Geosteering domain literature validates the baseline's exact estimator family and argues structurally for output-level multi-interpretation blending, but offers no new buildable estimator: (a) SPE-212544 (2023) applied Viterbi to a Bayesian state-space matrix (GR-vs-type-log correlation likelihood, prior-informed transitions) and beat a residual-minimizing automated geosteering algorithm — comparable in 59% and significantly better in 34% of 1,440 synthetic laterals (relative performance only, no absolute RMSE on real wells); (b) the SPE Journal 'PluRaListic' geosteering robot's interpretation engine is a likelihood-weighted particle filter assimilating ONLY gamma-ray, confirming the baseline's GR-only PF arm matches state of practice with the competition's exposed channels; (c) its companion paper (arXiv:2402.06377 / Comput. Geosci. 2025) shows the GR-to-stratigraphy inversion is ill-posed — the best-GR-match particle does NOT recover true boundaries — which is the formal argument for retaining multiple interpretations (output blending) over any single MAP estimate. This independently endorses the one axis that has transferred on the hidden LB (PF-GBM output blending) rather than opening a new one.
findings.evidence: SPE-212544 abstract verbatim: 'comparable performance in 59% of laterals, and significantly improved performance in 34% of laterals.' arXiv:2402.06377 measurement model: state = boundary positions, observations = gamma-ray log only, Gaussian likelihood sigma=0.2; verbatim: 'multiple possible realizations of the reservoir boundaries that result in similar gamma-ray observations... emphasizes the need to consider multiple particles to deal with the ill-posed inverse problem.' All synthetic-trial evidence; vendor self-comparison in SPE-212544 (~7% of laterals unaccounted, presumably worse).

findings.claim: Two screened domain papers are dead ends for this competition, and the competition's data-channel constraint is independently re-confirmed: (a) arXiv:2103.05384 (LM-EnRML ensemble inversion for geosteering) conditions exclusively on density and Deep-EM resistivity logs via physics forward simulators plus a prior layered geomodel — zero occurrences of gamma/GR in the full text — so it is not buildable with ROGII's channels; (b) arXiv:2510.07564 (single-GRU wellbore trajectory-angle prediction) contains no ensembling, blending, PF, Viterbi, cross-correlation, or kriging content and addresses a different task; (c) test horizontal-well CSVs expose exactly MD,X,Y,Z,GR,TVT_input while train adds ANCC/ASTNU/ASTNL/EGFDU/EGFDL/BUDA/TVT — formation-surface columns are train-only, verified both in Deotte's notebook text and by direct local file inspection (/home/swatson/work/kaggle/ROGII/data/raw/test/000d7d20__horizontal_well.csv).
findings.evidence: Full-text greps: 2103.05384 has 0 hits for gamma/GR/particle/Viterbi/kriging, 74 hits for 'ensemble' (LM-EnRML smoother only); 2510.07564 has 0 hits for particle/blend/Viterbi/HMM/Kalman/kriging. Local test CSV header verified: MD,X,Y,Z,GR,TVT_input.

findings.claim: Leaderboard probing offers no path to hidden-set gains for this competition: the canonical Whitehill (AAAI-W 2018) attack is structurally specific to log-loss classification (per-example metric decomposition over a finite label space enabling c^m exhaustive label inference) and describes no method for continuous-target RMSE; moreover, even where probing worked, the advantage did not transfer — the attacker's rank collapsed from #4 to 225/848 once scoring moved from the probed 512-example subset to the full held-out test set. Note for completeness: RMSE-oracle probing literature does exist (Xie, arXiv:1802.04947, leaks submission-truth inner products), but it extracts public-split information only, which by the same transfer logic yields nothing on ROGII's hidden split — consistent with the strategy already in use (LB probes spent only to map the PF blend-weight curve, not to extract labels).
findings.evidence: PDF text extracted and verified verbatim: 'the log-loss can be computed for each example separately... exhaustive search over all possible c^m labelings'; 'our ranking declined precipitously at this point (to 225th place out of 848 contenders) since our guesses on the remaining 75% examples were just 1/c.' Zero occurrences of RMSE/regression in the paper.

result.caveats: Quantified-gain coverage is thin where it matters most: the single most actionable in-competition technique (ravaghi's exponential fade-in, tau=85) ships with NO disclosed score delta (kernel outputs stripped), so its gain on a strong stack is unknown and must be gated locally; note also the baseline's GBM arm may already implicitly learn fade-in behavior from md_since-like features, in which case the post-hoc transform adds nothing. All cross-domain blend evidence (ridge-beats-hill-climbing, FWLS +0.5%) comes from classification/rank-metric competitions, not RMSE regression — direction transfers, magnitudes and the rank transform do not. The two strongest geosteering results (SPE-212544 Viterbi, PF ill-posedness) are measured on synthetic trials only, and SPE-212544 is a paywalled vendor self-comparison with ~7% of laterals unaccounted. Two findings rest on split votes: flat-beats-slope (2-1, revealed preference rather than head-to-head) and the Successive-Halving theory (2-1, oracle-hyperparameter and i.i.d.-sample assumptions that a deterministic Kaggle LB probe violates); a related claim bounding correction gains at sub-1% for strong models was REFUTED, so no gain ceiling should be inferred from that paper. Five claims were refuted in adversarial verification (listed in the input), including the safety guarantee for affine recalibration and the hill-climbing-overfits-CV claim — do not cite those. Time-sensitivity: the competition is live (today 2026-06-11); sub-7 leaders remain unpublished and a method disclosure or new notebook could appear any day, so the 'nothing published below ~7.8' finding has a short shelf life. Per-well adaptive weighting brushes against an already-nulled gate (per-well selector routing); only the soft-weight FWLS form with heavy regularization is genuinely untried.
openQuestions: What is the actual LB score of ravaghi's hill-climbing + fade-in notebook, and does the fade-in (tau=85) contribute positive delta on a stack whose GBM already consumes md_since-style features — i.e., does the transform survive an OOF gate on the 8.131 stack?

Do the sub-7 leaders use a fundamentally different estimator (e.g., a physics forward model or learned alignment) or just better blending/post-processing of the same PF/GBM family? No disclosure exists; the ~1.4 ft gap to 6.7 remains unexplained.

Would a heavily regularized ridge/FWLS meta-blend over the existing OOF prediction columns (PF arm, 6 GBMs) — selected WITHOUT trusting small OOF or public-LB deltas, per the S5E12 inversion lesson — transfer on this competition's hidden split, given that the simpler per-well hard selector already gated null?

Is there a principled way to size the remaining LB probe budget across candidate post-processing variants (fade-in tau, drift shrinkage alpha) given that each probe is a deterministic aggregate RMSE on a fixed public split rather than an i.i.d. sample, which breaks the Successive-Halving assumptions?

refuted.claim: Post-training output corrections yield large gains only for weak/under-optimized base models (Autoformer: 16.64%±6.72% avg MSE improvement, up to 34.88% on ETTh1) but sub-1% gains for already-strong models (DLinear 0.73%±0.40%, Crossformer 0.58%±0.51%, SegRNN 0.87%±0.46%) — implying that for a heavily tuned stack like the 8.131 baseline, output-level affine/trend corrections should be expected to move RMSE by well under 1%, not by the paper's 30% headline.

The notebook discloses no blending, smoothing, particle-filter, or other post-processing techniques beyond simple fold-model averaging of the 5 GroupKFold models — it contains nothing attributable to the unpublished sub-7 leaders and no technique beyond what the 8.131 baseline already subsumes.

In Kaggle Playground S5E12, greedy hill-climbing ensemble weight selection overfit the CV objective: beyond a plateau (post-cutoff CV ~0.7088X, public LB 0.70722), every additional model improved CV while degrading the public leaderboard — direct evidence that greedy output-blend weight optimization on validation does not transfer to hidden test data.

Adaptive blend-weight models only transferred to the hidden test when extremely regularized: the winning FWLS used Weka LinearRegression with regularization set at 1000, and every more complex regression model for vote-weight prediction failed to improve the test score due to overfitting — direct evidence that per-instance blend weighting under hidden-test transfer demands heavy regularization of the meta-model.

Fitting an optimal affine correction y_corr = a*f(x) + b on validation data to a frozen model's predictions provably never increases expected MSE, with the risk reduction equal to the squared mean-bias plus a variance-mismatch term — i.e., per-model (or per-well) linear bias/scale re-calibration of outputs is a theoretically safe post-processing step in expectation, though the guarantee holds only in-distribution, not under validation-to-hidden-test shift.
